title: What are Java virtual threads and how do they improve concurrency?
description: Explain Project Loom's virtual threads in Java 21.
options: Virtual threads are lightweight threads managed by the JVM, not the OS. Created via Thread.ofVirtual() or Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor(). Millions can exist simultaneously (vs thousands of platform threads). They mount/unmount from carrier (platform) threads automatically, especially during blocking I/O. Ideal for I/O-bound workloads
correct_answer: Virtual threads are lightweight threads managed by the JVM, not the OS. Created via Thread.ofVirtual() or Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor(). Millions can exist simultaneously (vs thousands of platform threads). They mount/unmount from carrier (platform) threads automatically, especially during blocking I/O. Ideal for I/O-bound workloads

title: Implement a Java record-based Result type for error handling.

title: What are Java scoped values and how do they differ from ThreadLocal?
options: ScopedValue (JEP 446) provides immutable, inheritable context within a bounded scope. Unlike ThreadLocal (mutable, unbounded lifetime, memory leak risk), ScopedValue is immutable once bound, automatically cleaned up when scope exits, and efficiently inherited by child virtual threads. Usage: ScopedValue.where(KEY, value).run(() -> { KEY.get(); })
correct_answer: ScopedValue (JEP 446) provides immutable, inheritable context within a bounded scope. Unlike ThreadLocal (mutable, unbounded lifetime, memory leak risk), ScopedValue is immutable once bound, automatically cleaned up when scope exits, and efficiently inherited by child virtual threads. Usage: ScopedValue.where(KEY, value).run(() -> { KEY.get(); })

title: Implement a Java structured concurrency task scope.

title: What is the Java Stream Gatherer API?
description: Explain the Gatherer interface added as a preview in Java 22.
options: Gatherers (JEP 461) are custom intermediate stream operations, enabling transformations impossible with built-in operations. stream.gather(gatherer) processes elements with state, supports 1-to-many and many-to-1 mappings, and can short-circuit. Built-in: Gatherers.windowFixed(n), windowSliding(n), fold(), scan(), mapConcurrent(). Fills the gap between map/filter and collect
correct_answer: Gatherers (JEP 461) are custom intermediate stream operations, enabling transformations impossible with built-in operations. stream.gather(gatherer) processes elements with state, supports 1-to-many and many-to-1 mappings, and can short-circuit. Built-in: Gatherers.windowFixed(n), windowSliding(n), fold(), scan(), mapConcurrent(). Fills the gap between map/filter and collect

title: What is the difference between Java's SequencedCollection interfaces?
description: Explain SequencedCollection, SequencedSet, and SequencedMap in Java 21.
options: Java 21 added SequencedCollection (ordered with defined encounter order), SequencedSet (unique + ordered), and SequencedMap (key-value + ordered). New methods: getFirst(), getLast(), addFirst(), addLast(), reversed(). LinkedList, ArrayList, TreeSet, LinkedHashSet, LinkedHashMap, TreeMap now implement these. Fills the API gap where accessing first/last elements required workarounds
correct_answer: Java 21 added SequencedCollection (ordered with defined encounter order), SequencedSet (unique + ordered), and SequencedMap (key-value + ordered). New methods: getFirst(), getLast(), addFirst(), addLast(), reversed(). LinkedList, ArrayList, TreeSet, LinkedHashSet, LinkedHashMap, TreeMap now implement these. Fills the API gap where accessing first/last elements required workarounds
